title: 6.7 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 6.7!
topic_path: The First Global Age (1200 to 1750 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Mercator map influence the way people perceive global power and importance?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Enlarging regions near the poles made European and North American countries appear more dominant.
By accurately showing all countries to scale, it promoted equal global representation.
Minimizing landmasses in the Southern Hemisphere encouraged increased trade with Africa and South America.
By centering the map on the Pacific Ocean, it shifted focus away from Europe and toward Asia.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did joint-stock companies play in the expansion of European exploration and colonization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They reduced investors' financial risk when funding voyages which helped expand overseas exploration.
They provided military forces for European monarchs to conquer colonies directly.
They replaced monarchs as the primary decision-makers in European governments.
They were created mainly to regulate local trade between European nations so government officials could focus overseas.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the economic goals of early European exploration evolve during the Age of Exploration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European nations shifted from seeking new trade routes to Asia to controlling global trade networks.
Europeans stopped focusing on overseas trade and turned to developing local economies instead.
The main goal changed from resource extraction to spreading democratic ideals.
European explorers established peaceful trade alliances with indigenous peoples instead of taking land.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which explanation best captures the meaning of "gunpowder empires"?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Empires that expanded their power mainly by using advanced firearms and cannons.
Empires that relied on peaceful trade and diplomacy to grow.
Empires that were founded during the Industrial Revolution.
Empires known for their use of traditional swords and bows in battle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did gunpowder empires use bureaucracies to strengthen their rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By creating organized systems to collect taxes and manage government affairs.
By giving citizens a voice in government affairs through elected representatives.
By eliminating all forms of administration in favor of military control.
By allowing religious leaders complete control over government decisions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the gunpowder empires manage their religiously diverse populations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By aligning with religious officials and expressing tolerance of minority religions.
By removing all religious influence from their governments and enforcing complete secularism.
By isolating religious minorities and banning them from participating in government or trade.
By forcing all citizens to convert to the state religion through strict laws and punishments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did imperial rivalries change during the period 1450–1750?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European empires began competing for control of global trade routes and overseas colonies.
Empires ended rivalries and began cooperating to form a global alliance.
Most empires stopped expanding and focused only on developing their own territories.
Imperial rivalries were limited to religious disagreements, not territory or resources.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following 15th-century developments helped make 16th-century European exploration possible?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Advances in shipbuilding and navigation tools improved long-distance sea travel.
The Industrial Revolution increased the need for global markets.
The fall of the Roman Empire opened up trade across Europe.
The discovery of steam-powered engines allowed faster ocean crossings.

assessment_items.question_content:
>They come from the sea with strange weapons and loud thunder. They do not ask for permission, and they take what they desire. They claim the land is theirs, though they do not know it. We wonder, do they bring peace, or will they bring the end of our way of life?  

>— Anonymous Indigenous Observer, 16th century

**Based on the passage above, how did at least some non-Europeans view European exploration during the 16th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European exploration was seen as an aggressive intrusion that threatened Indigenous lands and ways of life.
European exploration was understood as a peaceful effort to share culture and knowledge.
European exploration was viewed as a respectful exchange of goods and traditions.
European exploration was seen as an opportunity for Indigenous peoples to expand their own empires across the ocean.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the Ottoman Empire’s role in global trade during the 1500s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It controlled key land and sea routes between Europe and Asia, profiting from trade taxes and tolls.
It avoided participation in long-distance trade and focused solely on farming and local markets.
It relied entirely on European merchants to manage trade within its borders.
It dominated transatlantic trade and built colonies in the Americas to control silver and spice routes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did diaspora communities play in global trade during the 1500s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They built trade networks that connected different regions and cultures.
They avoided contact with local populations and kept their trade practices secret.
They discouraged long-distance trade and promoted self-sufficiency in local economies.
They were primarily made up of explorers and conquerors.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What made Armenian merchants valuable to European empires?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They connected European markets with trade networks across Asia and the Middle East.
They conquered new lands on behalf of European powers.
They focused only on local trade within European cities.
They refused to work with non-Christian merchants, limiting trade contacts.
